edus.text: A few months ago ,
when I told friends and media colleagues
that I was interested in the Common Core State Standards ,
the most common response was `` What 's that ? ''
Now ,
it seems ,
everyone has an opinion about the Core .
And right now , opinions about the K-12 learning goals for math and English
that have spread nearly nationwide
are trending toward the heated .
While the school year is winding down ,
education policy sure is n't .
This past week brought a bunch of front-page news on the Common Core .
On May 30 , South Carolina Gov . Nikki Haley signed a law
requiring the state to stop using the Common Core after the upcoming school year .
And last week , Oklahoma dropped the standards effective immediately ,
bringing the total number of states
embracing Common Core State Standards
down to 42 , from a high of 45
( Indiana is the third state
to have pulled back ) .
Those states
that adopted ,
and then dropped , the Core
now face spending tens of millions of dollars
to create new standards ,
adopt new materials
to go with them
and retrain teachers .
Speak
ng of millions of dollars ,
he money behind the Common Core was the topic of a long story in The Washington Post yesterday
hat focused on Microsoft founder Bill Gates ' role in the creation of the standards and in encouraging their implementation .
he story detailed
ow the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation has spent some $ 200 million on the development of the standards , political lobbying , and grants to organizations
hat now support the Core .
The Gates Foundation is also a longtime supporter of NPR ,
ncluding its coverage of education . )
ducation standards are not a new idea .
hey 've been advocated in the United States at least since the 1950s .
ut our unique system of highly localized control of public schools with limited federal involvement in education has prevented them from getting much traction on a sustained , national level .
ntil now .
s the piece , by Lyndsey Layton , details ,
ates ' money helped unite disparate interests behind a single policy in an incredibly short amount of time .
` The Gates Foundation spread money across the political spectrum , to entities
ncluding the big teachers unions , the American Federation of Teachers and the National Education Association , and business organizations such as the U.S. Chamber of Commerce
groups
hat have clashed in the past
ut became vocal backers of the standards . `` Money flowed to policy groups on the right and left ,
unding research by scholars of varying political persuasions
ho promoted the idea of common standards .
iberals at the Center for American Progress and conservatives
ffiliated with the American Legislative Exchange Council
ho routinely disagree on nearly every issue
ccepted Gates money

nd found common ground on the Common Core . '' What has seemed most troubling for critics of the Core , and of the influence of large philanthropies in U.S. policy generally , is the close association between the Gates Foundation and the U.S. Department of Education under the Obama administration .
he Common Core are not , strictly speaking , national standards .
hey were developed independently of the federal government ,
nd states are not under a mandate
o adopt them .
ut the standards received a big boost in the form of funding incentives from the Obama administration .
ehind the alignment of interests , the Post article noted several close ties :
argot Rogers ,
ho was Education Secretary Arne Duncan 's chief of staff ,
nd James Shelton , now a deputy secretary , both came directly from the foundation .
he administration waived ethics rules
o allow the two of them to consult closely with former colleagues .
nd Chicago received $ 20 million in Gates funding
o reorganize schools
hile Duncan was that district 's CEO
efore leaving for his Cabinet position .
e reached out to the foundation yesterday for comment on the article and on the developments in Oklahoma and South Carolina ,
ut did n't hear back .
ome of the frustrations with the adoption of the Common Core reflect broader concerns with education policymaking in general .
n an ideal world , policies would be made like this :
ractitioners in the field would develop solutions to problems .
isinterested experts would study and test them .
hilanthropists would support that research and development phase
ithout picking winners .
nd then politicians , through the democratic process , would make the case to the public
o support the spread and implementation of the best identified solutions ,
hile giving practitioners the leeway
hey need
o continue to refine and propose new ones .
any in the ed-policy world agree :
he Common Core State Standards skipped a few key steps here .
ritics have long noted
hat the influence of classroom teachers
n writing the standards
as limited .
hey were n't pilot-tested ,
lthough , in fairness , it 's pretty hard to pilot universal standards -
ither they 're universal
r they 're not .
hey were adopted with little public debate .
nd their implementation ,
s we 've reported ,
as been plagued with criticisms :
otably that they are top-down , rushed and underfunded so far .
nd yet in some states , the Common Core built on earlier foundations
f setting high standards for students .
any teachers and communities have embraced them .
he bigger-picture problem may be one
hat Gates himself outlined to the Post .
` The guys
ho search for oil ,
hey spend a lot of money
esearching new tools , ''
ates said .
` Medicine - they spend a lot of money
inding new tools .
oftware is a very R and D-oriented industry .
he funding , in general , of what works in education ... is tiny .
t 's the lowest in this field than any field of human endeavor .
et you could argue
t should be the highest . ''
nd so ,
he billionaire said ,
hen he was approached with what sounded like a very good idea ,
e threw his support behind it .
ut
hen it comes to conceiving and implementing new education policies ,
ill Gates himself says ,
e is crying out for some competition .
